bfs.overview: Breadth-first expansion by layers. In unweighted mazes, it always finds the shortest path.
cons: Memory grows quickly with frontier size.

dijkstra.overview: Expands nodes by minimum accumulated cost. Works for weighted and unweighted mazes with non-negative weights.
cons: Usually slower than A* with good heuristic.

aStar.overview: Combines traveled cost and heuristic estimate to guide search toward goal efficiently.
pros: Often much faster than uninformed search.
Can remain optimal with admissible heuristic.
aStar.timeComplexity: Typical: O((V + E) log V), worst-case exponential

dfs.overview: Depth-first traversal prioritizing deep exploration before backtracking.
cons: May waste time in long dead ends.

iddfs.overview: Runs DFS repeatedly with increasing depth limits until goal is reached.
cons: Can be slower than BFS in practice.

bellmanFord.overview: Relaxes all edges repeatedly to compute shortest paths, robust on graphs with difficult weight patterns.
cons: Usually overkill for regular maze grids.

bidirectionalBfs.overview: Runs BFS from both start and goal and meets in the middle.
pros: Preserves shortest-path guarantee in unweighted graphs.

bidirectionalDijkstra.overview: Dijkstra from both ends to reduce search space on weighted scenarios.

kShortest.overview: Computes multiple candidate shortest paths instead of only one.
kShortest.timeComplexity: Typically O(K * V * (E + V log V))

floydWarshall.overview: All-pairs shortest path dynamic programming over full graph matrix.
pros: Answers any source-target query quickly after preprocessing.
cons: Very high O(n^3) runtime and O(n^2) memory.
Not practical for large maze graphs.

johnson.overview: Reweights edges then runs Dijkstra from multiple sources for efficient sparse all-pairs shortest path.
cons: Often unnecessary for single query solve.

spfa.overview: Queue-based shortest path relaxation variant, often fast on some sparse instances.
pros: Can be practical on favorable graphs.

uniformCost.overview: Expands least-cost frontier first, equivalent to Dijkstra when represented as graph search.
cons: Can explore many states without heuristic.
Queue operations dominate on large maps.

deadEndFill.overview: Iteratively removes dead-end corridors until only solution backbone remains.
cons: Less suitable for heavily loopy mazes.

floodFill.overview: Spreads reachable labels from source across connected region, then reconstructs path.
pros: Simple and robust on grid mazes.
Good for visual debugging and education.
cons: Can be slower than guided search.

wallFollower.overview: Follows one wall continuously (left/right hand), relying on local geometry.
pros: Minimal state and easy control logic.
cons: Fails or loops in many non-simply-connected layouts.

pledge.overview: Wall-following with turn-sum compensation to escape loops around obstacles.
pros: Handles some loop traps better than pure wall-following.

randomMouse.overview: Chooses random valid moves until reaching goal; stochastic baseline behavior.

tremaux.overview: Marks visited corridors to avoid repeated mistakes; classic backtracking navigation method.
cons: Can still be longer than shortest path.

lee.overview: Grid wave propagation method equivalent to shortest-path BFS in orthogonal grids.
pros: Guarantees shortest path in uniform grids.

chainAlgorithm.overview: Builds and extends chained frontier structures to connect route candidates.

dfsWallTracer.overview: Hybrid of depth-first exploration and wall tracing to bias traversal order.
cons: Behavior is sensitive to local geometry.

recursiveBacktrackingSolver.overview: Recursive DFS-style solver with explicit backtracking points.
cons: May follow deep wrong branches first.

wavefront.overview: Wave expansion from target/source to create descending gradient for path extraction.

anytimeAStar.overview: Anytime variant of A*: quickly returns a feasible path, then improves it as time allows.

beamSearch.overview: Heuristic search that keeps only top-k candidates at each layer.
pros: Very memory-efficient with small beam width.
Can be fast in constrained settings.
cons: Quality sensitive to beam width tuning.
beamSearch.timeComplexity: O(w * d) with beam width w, depth d

bestFirst.overview: Expands node with best heuristic score first (greedy orientation).
pros: Often quickly reaches a feasible path.
cons: Can be trapped by misleading heuristic.

dStarLite.overview: Incremental heuristic replanning for dynamic environments with changing costs.
cons: Overhead is high for static mazes.
dStarLite.timeComplexity: Per replan typically O((V + E) log V)

fringeSearch.overview: Memory-lean A*-like method processing nodes in f-cost fringes.
pros: Can reduce memory pressure versus classic A*.
cons: Less common and harder to tune.
Performance profile depends on threshold policy.

greedyBfs.overview: Greedy best-first variant that prioritizes heuristic distance only.
pros: Usually very fast to find a path.
cons: Can take poor detours in deceptive layouts.

idaStar.overview: Depth-first iterative deepening over f-cost thresholds (A* idea with DFS memory).

jumpPointSearch.overview: Prunes symmetric moves in uniform-cost grids to accelerate A* search.
jumpPointSearch.timeComplexity: A*-like with strong pruning on grids

lifelongPlanningAStar.overview: Incremental A* family method that reuses previous search results after map changes.

thetaStar.overview: Any-angle extension of A* that can shorten paths by relaxing parent constraints.
pros: Can produce smoother and shorter practical routes.
Often better path quality than strict grid A*.

bug1.overview: Robot navigation strategy: move toward goal, circumnavigate obstacle fully, then leave at best point.

bug2.overview: Improves Bug1 by using a goal line (M-line) to leave obstacle boundaries earlier.
cons: Still can detour heavily in complex maps.

potentialField.overview: Uses attractive force to goal and repulsive force from obstacles to navigate.
pros: Very reactive and continuous navigation behavior.
cons: Can get trapped in local minima.

tangentBug.overview: Local obstacle-boundary reasoning with tangent information to improve Bug-style navigation.
pros: Better decisions than basic Bug methods in cluttered maps.

antColony.overview: Population of agents lays and follows pheromone trails to bias promising routes.

geneticSearch.overview: Evolutionary search over path populations using selection, crossover, and mutation.

qLearning.overview: Learns policy from reward feedback by updating action-value estimates over episodes.
cons: Sensitive to reward design and exploration policy.

simulatedAnnealing.overview: Probabilistic optimization that accepts occasional worse moves early to escape local minima.

classical.overview: Classical traversal methods prioritize deterministic movement rules and simplicity.

shortestPath.overview: Shortest-path family focuses on path optimality and strong correctness guarantees.
cons: Can be heavy on large graphs.

heuristic.overview: Heuristic methods trade exactness for speed by guiding exploration with estimates.
pros: Fast convergence in many practical maps.
cons: Result quality depends on heuristic quality.

navigation.overview: Navigation methods use local sensing/rules, often inspired by robotics.

metaheuristic.overview: Metaheuristics use stochastic/global optimization strategies over many iterations.
